claims.text: In 1979 the US Army launched 'First Earth Battalion' / Project Jedi — a real, declassified program that tried to build psychic soldiers and rewire warfare around meditation, intuition, and non-lethal techniques.

evidence.quote:
In 1979, the US
Army launched Project Jedi, a program to
build an army of psychic soldiers. They
were called the First Earth Battalion.

Shannon wrote it at Fort Levvenworth in
1979.

claims.rationale: The First Earth Battalion concept and the Army's exploration of related psychic-warfare programs (Stargate / Grill Flame / Center Lane / Sun Streak) are real and well documented in declassified CIA/Army records. Some of the labels and chronology the host gives are simplified, but the broad historical claim is accurate.

claims.text: Lieutenant Colonel Jim Channon — the FEB's creator — earned credibility because his Vietnam combat record was extraordinary: 319 days in combat in the Iron Triangle, only one soldier lost (to a sniper on day one), and zero civilian casualties under his command.

evidence.quote:
Shannon spent 319 days in combat. He led
search and destroy operations in
Vietnam. His unit was part of the 173rd
Airborne Brigade, one of the first major
army units deployed to Vietnam in 1965.
They operated in the Iron Triangle, one
of the war's deadliest zones.

but Shannon lost only one
soldier, and he was killed by a sniper
on his first day. For the next 317 days,
his platoon suffered zero casualties,
and they never killed a single innocent
civilian.

claims.rationale: Jim Channon (note the spelling — host says 'Shannon') was a real Army officer, served two tours in Vietnam with the 173rd Airborne, and authored the First Earth Battalion concept. The specific casualty figures (one death, zero civilians) come from Channon's own retelling and Jim Schnabel/Jon Ronson reporting; they are plausible but not independently verified to the precision claimed.

claims.text: Channon's First Earth Battalion field manual — written at Fort Leavenworth in 1979 and now publicly archived — outlined a 'warrior monk' soldier equipped with herbal supplements, dowsing rods, hand-to-hand pressure-point techniques from Tai Chi, mandatory daily hugs, biodegradable smart-fabric uniforms, and command centers built around isolation tanks and breathing exercises.

evidence.quote:
It contained things like
herbal supplements, mindfulness, music
used in combat. Soldiers were supposed
to hug every day to exchange energy.

Even the uniform was
unique. Biodegradable materials that
wouldn't harm the environment. built-in
solar panels for charging equipment and
colorchanging fabric that adapts to
surroundings. There are embedded health
sensors to monitor vital signs in real
time. And Shana described all this in
1979.

claims.rationale: The First Earth Battalion field manual (officially 'Evolutionary Tactics') really does cover all of these elements — warrior monk ethos, smart uniforms, hugging, biodegradable equipment, etc. The document is freely available online. Some details (the $7,600/gallon hand soap) may be embellishments but the core inventory is faithful.

claims.text: Major General Albert Stubblebine III — commander of US Army Intelligence (INSCOM) with 16,000 troops under him — was a true believer who repeatedly tried to walk through the wall of his Pentagon office and held mandatory spoon-bending parties for senior officers.
entities: organization:us army intelligence and security command

evidence.quote:
The
program's biggest champion was Major
General Albert Stubble III, West Point
graduate, master degree in chemical
engineering, 32 years of military
service. Stubble was commander of the US
Army Intelligence and Security Command,
INSCOM, responsible for all Army
Intelligence Operations around the
world. 16,000 soldiers under his
command

He stood in his office in
the Pentagon. He stared at the wall
across from his desk. He took a breath,
focused his mind, and ran straight into
the wall. He hit his head hard again and
again and again. Stubble believed he
could walk through walls.

hosted spoon bending parties.
High-ranking officers were required to
attend. They gathered in a room with
silverware. They stared at the metal
until it melted.

claims.rationale: Major General Albert Stubblebine III was real, did command INSCOM in the early 1980s, and was a documented true believer in psychic phenomena. The wall-walking attempts and spoon-bending parties are reported by him and by colleagues (including in Jon Ronson's 'The Men Who Stare at Goats'). Reasonable confidence the host's description is accurate.

claims.text: The CIA's Project Stargate remote-viewing program produced documented hits — Pat Price (subject 372) sketched a Soviet shipyard 4,000 miles away that satellite imagery later confirmed; Ingo Swann claimed to have seen Jupiter's rings before NASA discovered them; Joe McMoneagle earned a Legion of Merit for his remote-viewing intelligence work.

evidence.quote:
One remote viewer code
named subject 372 was given coordinates,
nothing else. He described a large
building with a [music] distinctive
curved roof. He said it was near water.
He drew a sketch that showed unusual
architectural features. The target was a
Soviet shipyard 4,000 mi away. His
drawing matched satellite photos taken 3
days later.

Subject 372 [music] was Pat Price, a
former police commissioner from
California who became one of the most
gifted remote viewers in the program.

Ingo Swan, another
legendary viewer, claimed he could see
Jupiter's rings before NASA discovered
them. Joe McMonicle spent years remote
viewing Soviet facilities. His accuracy
was so consistent, he received a legion
of merit for his intelligence work.

claims.rationale: Pat Price, Ingo Swann, and Joe McMoneagle are all real, all worked in the SRI/Stargate program, and the famous-hit anecdotes are widely retold. McMoneagle did receive the Legion of Merit. However, declassified evaluations (the 1995 American Institutes for Research review) judged that the cited successes do not exceed reasonable hit rates after accounting for sensory leakage and post-hoc matching. The events are real; their evidentiary value for psi is the contested part.

claims.text: The CIA closed the remote-viewing program in 1995 after concluding it had not been proven to work by any psychic mechanism — but the personnel and the broader First Earth Battalion philosophy were absorbed into mainstream special-operations training.

evidence.quote:
But in 1995, the CIA
reviewed the entire program. [music]
They said that remote viewing had not
been proven to work by a psychic
mechanism, so they shut it down
allegedly.

But the people didn't disappear. Major
General Stubble retired and founded
[music] Scitec, a private remote viewing
company.

But the techniques from
Shannon's manual grounded in physics and
psychology were quietly absorbed into
standard military training.

claims.rationale: The 1995 closure of the Stargate program after the AIR review and the founding of PEAR-adjacent / private remote-viewing companies (Stubblebine's PSI Tech, etc.) is documented. The wider absorption of mindfulness, breathing, and stress-control techniques into special-operations training is also true (e.g., Mind Fitness Training pilot programs, USSOCOM Cognitive Performance initiatives).

claims.text: The Army's psychotronic-sound weapon work culminated in the December 1989 siege of Manuel Noriega in Panama, where instead of opening fire US forces blasted Van Halen and The Clash at deafening volumes for three days until he surrendered.

evidence.quote:
In 1989, the army
tested Shannon's psychotronic sound
weapons during Operation Just Cause.
They surrounded Manuel Noriega's
compound in Panama. They didn't shoot.
They set up massive speakers and played
Van Halen and the Clash at deafening
volumes for three days straight. He
surrendered.

claims.rationale: Operation Nifty Package (Dec 1989-Jan 1990) really did surround Noriega in the Vatican Embassy in Panama City and blast loud rock music to pressure him out. The 'three days straight' figure is reasonable. Calling it 'Shannon's psychotronic sound weapon' is a stretch — the sound siege wasn't a direct First Earth Battalion deliverable, but the lineage of non-lethal/PSYOPS thinking is genuine.

claims.text: Several genuinely funded but bizarre Air Force non-lethal weapons proposals — the 1994 Wright Lab 'halitosis bomb' for severe bad breath and the so-called 'gay bomb' to spray aphrodisiacs that would make enemy troops sexually attracted to each other — show how seriously the military pursued the FEB's non-lethal vision.

evidence.quote:
In 1994, the Air Force Right Laboratory
proposed a halattosis bomb, a chemical
weapon to give enemies severe bad
breath.

Then came the gay
bomb. They wanted to spray aphrodesiacs
over enemy troops to make them sexually
attracted to each other. It was a real
funded proposal.

claims.rationale: The 1994 Wright Laboratory non-lethal chemical-weapon proposal really did include a halitosis option and what came to be called the 'gay bomb' aphrodisiac. The proposal is in declassified records and won a satirical Ig Nobel in 2007. The host's claim is essentially correct; whether to credit the FEB lineage is more interpretive.

claims.text: The host's verdict: the literal psychic-soldier promises of the First Earth Battalion failed (no walls walked, goats killed by accident not psi, remote viewing inconclusive), but the underlying program was vindicated — meditation, mindfulness, situational awareness, non-lethal weapons, and 'hearts and minds' counterinsurgency are now mainstream Army practice.

evidence.quote:
But the failure of
psychic programs doesn't mean the first
Earth battalion was worthless. In high
stress situations, the human mind is
capable of far more than we realize.
Shannon's Vietnam platoon proved that
his soldiers weren't psychic. They were
just really well trained.

But here's what Jim
Shannon got right. War doesn't have to
be about maximum violence. Soldiers can
be trained to operate with precision,
awareness, and minimal collateral
damage. They can use technology to
achieve objectives [music] without
massive body counts. Today, that's not a
conspiracy theory. That's a fact.

claims.rationale: This split verdict matches the historical assessment of the FEB legacy. Modern USSOCOM training really does include mindfulness, breath control, and emotional-regulation modules; non-lethal options like sticky foam are standard; counterinsurgency doctrine genuinely centers hearts and minds. The host's calibration here is well taken.
